paragraphs.text: “And it came to pass at the end of two full years, that Pharaoh dreamed” (Gen 41:1). Pharaoh saw in his dream ears of grain of two kinds and cows of two kinds. To interpret this would not have been difficult for anyone; but in order that Joseph might be the one to interpret the dream, the meaning of the dream was hidden from Pharaoh’s wise men. And when the cupbearer after two years reminded Pharaoh of Joseph, Pharaoh sent and brought him out of prison. So the hair grown long through sorrow is shorn from Joseph by joy, and the unclean garment in which sadness had clothed him is taken off by gladness.

Joseph came to Pharaoh, heard the dreams, and saw the calamity that threatened the Egyptians. Then, having revealed the true meaning of the dreams, he added useful counsel to Pharaoh: “Look out a man discreet and wise, and set him over the land of Egypt… and let them gather all the food of those good years… and that food shall be… against the seven years of famine… that the land perish not through the famine” (Gen 41:33, 35–36). When Joseph said to Pharaoh, “Look out a man,” he meant himself, but out of modesty did not name himself. Nor did he point to anyone else, for he knew that no one except himself could deliver the Egyptians from the great calamity that would come upon them.

Joseph rose in Pharaoh’s eyes by his interpretation of the dreams, and especially by the useful counsel which he gave. And Pharaoh entrusted to Joseph authority over his whole kingdom, and gave him the ring with which the royal treasures were sealed. What had never been placed upon the hand of any Egyptian, Pharaoh in the sight of all the people set on Joseph’s finger. And with the ring given to him was also given authority over all things. “I am Pharaoh; without thee shall no man lift up his hand in all the land of Egypt” (Gen 41:44). So, together with everything else that was subjected to Joseph, the military commanders and the royal nobles were also subjected to him.

When Joseph was interpreting the dreams for Pharaoh, his former master was there too. And seeing that his former slave was less than Pharaoh only by the royal throne, he went to his house with the same haste with which he had earlier gone to his wife coming to meet him to slander Joseph. And he says to his wife: “Joseph, who was our slave, has now become our master. Him whom we cast into prison without clothing, Pharaoh has clothed in purple. Him whom we cast among prisoners now sits upon Pharaoh’s chariot. And he who was bound with iron bonds, on his neck has been placed a golden chain. How shall I now turn my gaze and look on him on whom I dare not even raise my eyes?” But his wife says to him: “Fear not him to whom thou hast done no evil. He knows whether the dishonor that came upon him when he was cast out of our house was just or unjust, because he received it from my hands. Go now without fear with the other nobles and commanders who escort his chariot, that Joseph may not think his present greatness grieves us. And in proof that Joseph is not evil, contrary to my former lie, I will now tell all the truth. I myself loved Joseph when I slandered him; I myself held him by his garment, because I was overcome by his beauty. If he loves justice, he may take vengeance on me, not on thee. But of course, out of his love of justice he will not take vengeance even on me, because, if he had not been slandered, he would not have been cast into prison. And if he had not been in prison, he would not have interpreted Pharaoh’s dreams and would not have attained the greatness of which thou tellest me. Although it was not we who raised him to this height, yet we as it were contributed to it, because the humiliation into which he was brought by us was the cause of his being glorified and becoming second after the king.”

Potiphar went and with the other nobles escorted Joseph’s chariot through the streets. But Joseph did him no harm. For he knew that He who had permitted his brothers to cast him into the pit in the wilderness, and from the pit in bonds to send him into Egypt, had permitted Potiphar also to cast him among the prisoners, that out of this humiliation He might raise him to Pharaoh’s chariot.

Joseph began to gather grain and gathered yearly in all the cities of Egypt. When the abundant years had passed and the years of famine were come, then he showed his care for the orphans, widows, and all the poor who were in Egypt, leaving no one without help. And if the famine had been only in Egypt, Egypt would have had nothing to fear, because much grain had been gathered by Joseph. But the famine was over all the earth, and all had need of Egyptian grain. And from this even to the Egyptians themselves grain was now sold at a high price. And Scripture, showing that the famine was universal, says: “And all the lands came into Egypt to Joseph to buy; because the famine was sore in all the earth” (Gen 41:57).
